quiz.question: The report said the drug was freely obtainable without a prescription. What does 'obtainable' mean?
options: Possible to get or acquire
Available at reduced cost through a subsidy
Approved for sale by the relevant authority
Stocked by most pharmacies across the country
quiz.answer: Possible to get or acquire
quiz.explanation:
Obtainable means able to be obtained — possible to get or acquire.
• obtainable = capable of being got or procured
• From 'obtain' — to get, from Latin 'obtinere'
• 'Freely obtainable' = no barriers to getting it

quiz.question: Littering in the park was a finable offense. What does 'finable' mean?
options: Subject to a financial penalty
Recorded by officers with a warning issued
Punishable by community service
Reported to the local council for action
quiz.answer: Subject to a financial penalty
quiz.explanation:
Finable means liable to a fine — subject to a monetary penalty.
• finable = capable of being fined for the offense
• From 'fine' — a monetary penalty imposed as punishment
• 'A finable offense' = one for which a fine can be issued

quiz.question: The interminable meeting dragged on through the entire afternoon. What does 'interminable' mean?
options: Seemingly endless and exhaustingly long
Poorly organized with no clear agenda
Dominated by one person refusing to yield the floor
Repeatedly rescheduled before finally taking place
quiz.answer: Seemingly endless and exhaustingly long
quiz.explanation:
Interminable means seemingly endless — so long it appears to have no end.
• interminable = endless; tiresomely long
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'terminare' (to end)
• 'An interminable wait', 'interminable speech' — it goes on and on

quiz.question: The judge called the landlord's behavior unconscionable — charging extortionate rent for uninhabitable conditions. What does 'unconscionable' mean?
options: Unreasonably excessive and far beyond what is morally acceptable
Illegal and in clear breach of tenancy law
Exploitative of vulnerable people in desperate situations
Deliberately designed to trap tenants in an unfair contract, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Unreasonably excessive and far beyond what is morally acceptable
quiz.explanation:
Unconscionable means shockingly unreasonable — far beyond what any conscience would permit.
• unconscionable = beyond any reasonable standard; shockingly unfair
• From 'unconscionable' — acting against conscience
• 'Unconscionable behavior' shocks the moral sense

quiz.question: Parents were warned that the programme was unsuitable for impressionable young children. What does 'impressionable' mean?
options: Easily influenced and shaped by what one sees or experiences
Too young to understand the content being shown
Likely to be frightened or disturbed by strong imagery, as the evidence showed
Not yet mature enough to distinguish fact from fiction
quiz.answer: Easily influenced and shaped by what one sees or experiences
quiz.explanation:
Impressionable means easily influenced — especially by experiences and ideas.
• impressionable = easily molded by external influences
• From 'impression' — a mark or influence left on the mind
• Young people are described as impressionable because their views are still forming

quiz.question: The committee found nothing exceptionable in the report and approved it unanimously. What does 'exceptionable' mean?
options: Objectionable or open to criticism
Unusually good and deserving recognition
Exceptional in its depth and quality of analysis
Likely to provoke disagreement among members
quiz.answer: Objectionable or open to criticism
quiz.explanation:
Exceptionable means open to objection — giving grounds for criticism.
• exceptionable = objectionable; open to exception
• Often confused with 'exceptional' (outstanding) — they mean almost opposite things
• 'Nothing exceptionable' = nothing to object to

quiz.question: Her conduct was entirely unexceptionable — there was nothing anyone could complain about. What does 'unexceptionable' mean?
options: Perfectly proper and giving no grounds for objection
Extremely good and worthy of praise
Ordinary and therefore unlikely to attract notice, as the evidence showed
Correct according to the rules but nothing more
quiz.answer: Perfectly proper and giving no grounds for objection
quiz.explanation:
Unexceptionable means not open to objection — entirely proper and correct.
• unexceptionable = not objectionable; perfectly satisfactory
• Direct opposite of exceptionable
• Note: NOT the same as 'unexceptional' (mediocre) — 'unexceptionable' means blameless

quiz.question: The candidate was highly personable and created a strong impression in the interview. What does 'personable' mean?
options: Having a pleasant manner and agreeable appearance
Confident and articulate when speaking in public, as the evidence showed
Professionally polished and well-prepared
Warm and easy to build a rapport with
quiz.answer: Having a pleasant manner and agreeable appearance
quiz.explanation:
Personable means having a pleasing personality and appearance — attractive in manner.
• personable = having a pleasant appearance and friendly manner
• From 'person' — relating to the whole presentation of a person
• 'A personable young man' = pleasant to meet and deal with

quiz.question: There was an impalpable tension in the room that no one would name. What does 'impalpable' mean?
options: Impossible to perceive directly but still felt
Hidden beneath a surface of forced politeness, as the evidence showed
Too vague to be described in precise terms
Present but denied by everyone who was there
quiz.answer: Impossible to perceive directly but still felt
quiz.explanation:
Impalpable means not able to be touched or perceived directly — intangible.
• impalpable = unable to be felt or grasped; imperceptible
• From Latin: 'im-' + 'palpare' (to touch)
• 'An impalpable atmosphere' = felt but not definable

quiz.question: The investigation found three managers culpable for the accident. What does 'culpable' mean?
options: Deserving blame and responsible for the harm caused
Formally charged with an offense relating to the accident
Identified as having failed to follow correct procedure
Named in the report as having contributed to the outcome
quiz.answer: Deserving blame and responsible for the harm caused
quiz.explanation:
Culpable means deserving blame — guilty of a fault or error.
• culpable = at fault; deserving censure
• From Latin: 'culpa' — fault, blame
• 'Culpable negligence' = negligence serious enough to warrant blame

quiz.question: However chaotic the situation became, she remained unflappable. What does 'unflappable' mean?
options: Incapable of being upset or flustered
Experienced enough not to be surprised by setbacks
Determined to stay professional regardless of circumstances
Trained to keep calm in high-pressure environments
quiz.answer: Incapable of being upset or flustered
quiz.explanation:
Unflappable means impossible to flap — remaining completely calm under pressure.
• unflappable = not easily upset or disturbed; imperturbably calm
• From 'flap' — to become agitated or flustered
• 'Unflappable composure' — the chaos around simply does not land

quiz.question: The valley contained some of the most productive arable land in the region. What is arable land?
options: Land suitable for growing crops by ploughing
Land with enough rainfall to sustain livestock
Land that has been cleared and drained for farming
Land owned and cultivated by a farming cooperative
quiz.answer: Land suitable for growing crops by ploughing
quiz.explanation:
Arable land is land that is fit for growing crops — ploughable and cultivable.
• arable = fit for growing crops; relating to crop farming
• From Latin: 'arabilis' — from 'arare' (to plough)
• 'Arable farming' = crops (wheat, barley, etc.); distinct from pastoral (livestock)

quiz.question: The teacher told the parable of the Prodigal Son to illustrate forgiveness. What is a parable?
options: A short story with a moral or spiritual lesson
A prayer recited to ask for divine guidance
A hymn used to teach a scriptural concept
An allegory in which every character represents a virtue
quiz.answer: A short story with a moral or spiritual lesson
quiz.explanation:
A parable is a simple story used to illustrate a moral or spiritual lesson.
• parable = a narrative from which a moral or spiritual truth can be drawn
• From Greek: 'parabolē' — a comparison, placing alongside
• The New Testament parables use everyday scenarios to teach deeper truths

quiz.question: The damage to the car was reparable — an expensive but manageable fix. What does 'reparable' mean?
options: Able to be repaired or made good
Covered by the warranty and fixable at no cost
Significant but not affecting the car's roadworthiness
Likely to cause further problems if not addressed quickly
quiz.answer: Able to be repaired or made good
quiz.explanation:
Reparable means capable of being repaired — the damage can be fixed.
• reparable = able to be repaired or remedied
• From Latin: 'reparare' — to restore
• Opposite: irreparable (cannot be fixed)

quiz.question: The hotel food was execrable — cold, flavorless, and barely edible. What does 'execrable' mean?
options: Extremely bad — of the very worst quality
Overpriced relative to the quality provided
Prepared with obvious indifference to the guests
So poor as to have generated multiple complaints
quiz.answer: Extremely bad — of the very worst quality
quiz.explanation:
Execrable means extremely bad — utterly horrible.
• execrable = extremely bad; deserving to be execrated
• From Latin: 'execrari' — to curse
• 'Execrable taste', 'execrable performance' — of the very worst kind

quiz.question: The renovation would require considerable time and money. What does 'considerable' mean?
options: Large enough to deserve attention and careful thought
More than originally estimated and increasingly likely to grow
Significant enough to affect the project's overall feasibility
Too large to be managed without external support
quiz.answer: Large enough to deserve attention and careful thought
quiz.explanation:
Considerable means large enough to be worthy of consideration — significant.
• considerable = notably large or important; worthy of thought
• From Latin: 'considerare' — to observe carefully
• 'A considerable sum', 'considerable effort' — not trivial; deserving attention

quiz.question: The philosopher said the question of consciousness was ponderable but perhaps not answerable. What does 'ponderable' mean?
options: Able to be deeply thought about or weighed mentally
Important enough to be taken seriously
Complex enough to occupy a lifetime of study
Interesting but ultimately beyond human understanding
quiz.answer: Able to be deeply thought about or weighed mentally
quiz.explanation:
Ponderable means capable of being weighed mentally — substantial enough to merit reflection.
• ponderable = able to be pondered; having appreciable mental weight
• From Latin: 'ponderare' — to weigh
• Opposite: imponderable (cannot be assessed or weighed)

quiz.question: The outcome of the election depended on imponderables that no model could capture. What are imponderables?
options: Factors too uncertain or complex to assess accurately
Variables that fluctuate too quickly to track reliably
Unknown events that could completely change the result
Emotional factors that resist rational analysis
quiz.answer: Factors too uncertain or complex to assess accurately
quiz.explanation:
Imponderables are things that cannot be precisely weighed or calculated — uncertainties beyond assessment.
• imponderable = not able to be estimated or accounted for
• From Latin: 'im-' + 'ponderare' (to weigh)
• 'The imponderables of human behavior' = the unmeasurable factors

quiz.question: His handwriting was indecipherable — even the author could not read it back. What does 'indecipherable' mean?
options: Impossible to read or make out
So small that it required magnification
Written in a personal shorthand only the writer knew
Faded to the point of illegibility over time
quiz.answer: Impossible to read or make out
quiz.explanation:
Indecipherable means impossible to decipher — impossible to read or decode.
• indecipherable = that cannot be deciphered; illegible or incomprehensible
• From 'decipher' — to convert from code or illegible form
• 'Indecipherable handwriting', 'indecipherable message' — cannot be decoded

quiz.question: Elderly residents were identified as particularly vulnerable during the heatwave. What does 'vulnerable' mean?
options: Exposed to risk of harm and lacking protection
Most likely to be seriously affected by extreme temperatures
Unable to access the support services they needed
At greater risk than others due to their age and health
quiz.answer: Exposed to risk of harm and lacking protection
quiz.explanation:
Vulnerable means exposed to harm — susceptible to damage, attack, or suffering.
• vulnerable = susceptible to injury or damage; open to attack
• From Latin: 'vulnerare' — to wound
• 'A vulnerable position', 'vulnerable populations' — lacking adequate protection

quiz.question: The general believed his position was invulnerable — impossible to attack from any direction. What does 'invulnerable' mean?
options: Impossible to harm or damage
Defended by enough forces to repel any attack
Located in terrain that made approach very difficult
Protected by fortifications that had never been breached
quiz.answer: Impossible to harm or damage
quiz.explanation:
Invulnerable means incapable of being wounded or harmed — completely safe from attack.
• invulnerable = not capable of being wounded or damaged
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'vulnerare' (to wound)
• Direct opposite of vulnerable

quiz.question: The project team faced insuperable obstacles and eventually abandoned the effort. What does 'insuperable' mean?
options: Impossible to overcome
More difficult than expected given available resources
Likely to become worse the longer they were left unaddressed
Requiring skills and expertise the team did not have
quiz.answer: Impossible to overcome
quiz.explanation:
Insuperable means impossible to overcome or surmount — an unconquerable obstacle.
• insuperable = incapable of being surmounted or solved
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'superare' (to surmount)
• 'Insuperable difficulty' — the barrier cannot be climbed over

quiz.question: Experience in project management was listed as desirable but not essential. What does 'desirable' mean?
options: Worth having and adding value if present
Preferred by the hiring panel over other qualities
Relevant to the role but not required by the job description
Likely to make the applicant more competitive
quiz.answer: Worth having and adding value if present
quiz.explanation:
Desirable means worth wanting — advantageous or appealing.
• desirable = worth having or wishing for; attractive
• From Latin: 'desiderare' — to long for
• In job ads: 'desirable' = a bonus, not a requirement

quiz.question: The council declared the site's current state undesirable and ordered cleanup. What does 'undesirable' mean?
options: Unwanted and falling below acceptable standards
Harmful to local residents and their property values
In breach of planning and environmental regulations
Likely to deteriorate further without immediate action
quiz.answer: Unwanted and falling below acceptable standards
quiz.explanation:
Undesirable means not wanted — harmful or unacceptable.
• undesirable = not desirable; offensive or harmful
• Opposite: desirable
• 'Undesirable effects', 'undesirable elements' — something one would rather not have

quiz.question: The mathematician confirmed that 12 was factorable. What does 'factorable' mean?
options: Able to be broken down into smaller factors
Even and therefore divisible by two
Expressible as a product of prime numbers
Divisible without remainder by at least three numbers
quiz.answer: Able to be broken down into smaller factors
quiz.explanation:
Factorable means able to be factored — expressible as a product of smaller numbers.
• factorable = capable of being factored
• From 'factor' — a number that divides evenly into another
• 12 = 2×6 = 3×4 = 2×2×3 — it has multiple factorings

quiz.question: The conditions were favorable for the launch, with calm winds and clear skies. What does 'favorable' mean?
options: Conducive to success and working in one's advantage
Within the safety parameters for the planned operation
Better than predicted by the meteorological forecast
Unlikely to change significantly in the next few hours
quiz.answer: Conducive to success and working in one's advantage
quiz.explanation:
Favorable means advantageous — expressing approval or supporting success.
• favorable = giving advantage; expressing approval
• From Latin: 'favor' — goodwill
• 'Favorable conditions', 'a favorable review' — things that help or support

quiz.question: The inexorable rise of digital media left traditional newspapers struggling to survive. What does 'inexorable' mean?
options: Impossible to stop or prevent — continuing relentlessly
Growing faster than any industry had predicted
Driven by forces too large for any individual to resist, as the evidence showed
Permanently transforming every sector it touches
quiz.answer: Impossible to stop or prevent — continuing relentlessly
quiz.explanation:
Inexorable means relentless — continuing without possibility of being stopped or persuaded.
• inexorable = impossible to stop or persuade; relentless
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'exorare' (to persuade by entreaty)
• 'The inexorable march of time' — nothing can halt or change it

quiz.question: The undergrowth was impenetrable — a wall of thorns and tangled roots. What does 'impenetrable' mean?
options: Impossible to pass through or gain access to
So dense that visibility was reduced to almost nothing
Too dangerous to enter without specialist equipment
Growing so rapidly it closed any gap cut through it
quiz.answer: Impossible to pass through or gain access to
quiz.explanation:
Impenetrable means impossible to penetrate — nothing can get through.
• impenetrable = not able to be entered or seen through
• From Latin: 'im-' + 'penetrare' (to enter)
• 'Impenetrable forest', 'impenetrable argument' — cannot be broken into

quiz.question: With automation advancing, many routine jobs were becoming dispensable. What does 'dispensable' mean?
options: Able to be removed without loss — not essential
Replaced by technology without any retraining required
Performed by machines at a fraction of the human cost
No longer needed as demand for such work was declining
quiz.answer: Able to be removed without loss — not essential
quiz.explanation:
Dispensable means able to be done without — not indispensable.
• dispensable = not essential; able to be removed or abandoned
• From 'dispense with' — to do without
• Opposite: indispensable (essential)

quiz.question: The surgeon put on disposable gloves before the procedure. What does 'disposable' mean?
options: Designed to be used once and then thrown away
Lightweight and easy to put on and remove quickly
Sterile and suitable for use in medical environments
Cheap enough to replace after every procedure
quiz.answer: Designed to be used once and then thrown away
quiz.explanation:
Disposable means designed for single use — to be thrown away after use.
• disposable = intended to be used once and discarded
• From 'dispose of' — to throw away
• 'Disposable income' = money available after essential expenses

quiz.question: The truth was unpalatable but had to be told — the project had failed completely. What does 'unpalatable' mean?
options: Difficult or unpleasant to accept
Controversial enough to provoke strong disagreement
Contrary to everything the team had been hoping for
Too damaging to be shared with external stakeholders
quiz.answer: Difficult or unpleasant to accept
quiz.explanation:
Unpalatable means difficult to accept — unpleasant to the mind or senses.
• unpalatable = not pleasant to accept; distasteful
• From 'palate' — literally unpleasant to the taste
• 'Unpalatable truth', 'unpalatable food' — both hard to swallow

quiz.question: She was a redoubtable opponent — no one relished arguing against her. What does 'redoubtable' mean?
options: Formidable and deserving respect or awe
Experienced and very difficult to outwit
Clever enough to turn any argument to her advantage
Aggressive and unwilling to concede any point
quiz.answer: Formidable and deserving respect or awe
quiz.explanation:
Redoubtable means formidable — inspiring respect or fear, especially as an opponent.
• redoubtable = causing fear or awe; formidable
• From Old French: 'redouter' — to fear greatly
• 'A redoubtable adversary' is someone you take very seriously

quiz.question: The new software made the complex data tractable for non-specialists. What does 'tractable' mean?
options: Able to be managed or dealt with effectively
Simplified enough for a general audience to use
Compatible with the systems already in use
Exportable into multiple standard formats
quiz.answer: Able to be managed or dealt with effectively
quiz.explanation:
Tractable means manageable — easily dealt with or controlled.
• tractable = easy to deal with or manage
• From Latin: 'tractare' — to handle
• Opposite: intractable (unmanageable)

quiz.question: The conflict had proven intractable — decades of negotiation had produced nothing. What does 'intractable' mean?
options: Impossible to manage or resolve
Rooted in historical grievances too deep to address
Involving too many parties with incompatible interests
Resistant to international mediation and pressure
quiz.answer: Impossible to manage or resolve
quiz.explanation:
Intractable means impossible to deal with or solve — unmanageable.
• intractable = hard or impossible to deal with; obstinate
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'tractare' (to handle)
• 'Intractable problem' has no solution; 'intractable person' refuses to cooperate

quiz.question: The guests were treated to a delectable array of desserts. What does 'delectable' mean?
options: Highly pleasing — especially to the taste
Beautifully presented and very appealing to the eye
Made from the finest seasonal and locally sourced ingredients
Prepared by a pastry chef of exceptional skill
quiz.answer: Highly pleasing — especially to the taste
quiz.explanation:
Delectable means highly pleasing — especially delicious to taste or delightful to experience.
• delectable = delightful; highly pleasing
• From Latin: 'delectare' — to delight
• 'Delectable food', 'a delectable view' — something that gives great pleasure

quiz.question: Death, he wrote, was ineluctable — no one and nothing could escape it. What does 'ineluctable' mean?
options: Impossible to avoid or escape
Universal in that it applies to all living things
Certain to come, however distant it may seem
Beyond the power of medicine or science to prevent
quiz.answer: Impossible to avoid or escape
quiz.explanation:
Ineluctable means unavoidable — impossible to escape or struggle against.
• ineluctable = not to be avoided; inevitable
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'eluctari' (to struggle free)
• 'Ineluctable fate', 'ineluctable conclusion' — cannot be wrestled away from

quiz.question: The planet was being studied to determine whether it was habitable. What does 'habitable' mean?
options: Capable of supporting life or being lived in
Close enough to its star to maintain liquid water
Similar enough to Earth to be explored by humans
Likely to have an atmosphere of suitable composition
quiz.answer: Capable of supporting life or being lived in
quiz.explanation:
Habitable means able to be lived in — suitable for human or biological habitation.
• habitable = suitable for living in
• From Latin: 'habitare' — to dwell
• 'Habitable conditions', 'habitable zone' — where life can be sustained

quiz.question: The evidence was indubitable — no jury could have reached any other verdict. What does 'indubitable' mean?
options: Too clear to be doubted
Supported by multiple independent sources
Consistent with every other piece of evidence in the case
Presented in such a way that no reasonable person could reject it
quiz.answer: Too clear to be doubted
quiz.explanation:
Indubitable means beyond doubt — absolutely certain and unquestionable.
• indubitable = that cannot be doubted; unquestionable
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'dubitare' (to doubt)
• 'Indubitably' = without a doubt — an emphatic affirmation

quiz.question: Her inimitable style made her impossible to copy and easy to recognize. What does 'inimitable' mean?
options: So unique that it cannot be copied or reproduced
So distinctive that it has become widely influential
So personal that any imitation would be immediately obvious
So refined that others lack the skill to replicate it
quiz.answer: So unique that it cannot be copied or reproduced
quiz.explanation:
Inimitable means impossible to imitate — uniquely distinctive.
• inimitable = defying imitation; matchlessly unique
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'imitari' (to imitate)
• 'The inimitable voice of...' = a quality so individual no copy could match it

quiz.question: The local people were extraordinarily hospitable, offering food and shelter to every visitor. What does 'hospitable' mean?
options: Welcoming and generous to guests and strangers
Known for their generosity and warmth as a community
Proud of their cultural tradition of welcoming travelers
Prepared to give their own comforts to help others
quiz.answer: Welcoming and generous to guests and strangers
quiz.explanation:
Hospitable means welcoming to guests — generous in providing food, shelter, and warmth.
• hospitable = giving a warm and generous reception
• From Latin: 'hospites' — host, guest (same word for both)
• 'Hospitable climate' = one conducive to life; 'hospitable people' = welcoming

quiz.question: She gave the most charitable interpretation of his remark, assuming he meant no harm. What does 'charitable' mean?
options: Generous and inclined to think the best of others
Forgiving enough to overlook minor social offenses
Kind enough to excuse behavior that others might resent
Sympathetic to someone who may have acted thoughtlessly
quiz.answer: Generous and inclined to think the best of others
quiz.explanation:
Charitable means generous — both in giving and in judging others favorably.
• charitable = generous; inclined to be kind in interpreting others' actions
• From Latin: 'caritas' — love, esteem
• 'A charitable interpretation' = the most favorable possible reading

quiz.question: The speech contained several notable passages that were quoted widely. What does 'notable' mean?
options: Worthy of attention — remarkable or significant
Eloquent and well-constructed throughout
Unusual enough to have surprised those who heard it
Likely to be remembered as historically significant
quiz.answer: Worthy of attention — remarkable or significant
quiz.explanation:
Notable means worthy of notice — remarkable, significant, or deserving attention.
• notable = worthy of note; remarkable
• From Latin: 'notare' — to mark
• 'A notable exception', 'a notable achievement' — stands out and deserves mention

quiz.question: The expedition carried enough potable water for three weeks. What does 'potable' mean?
options: Safe and clean enough to drink
Treated and purified for human consumption
Stored in containers suitable for drinking water
Sufficient for drinking and basic hygiene
quiz.answer: Safe and clean enough to drink
quiz.explanation:
Potable means fit to drink — safe and clean enough for human consumption.
• potable = drinkable; safe to drink
• From Latin: 'potare' — to drink
• 'Potable water' = water that meets drinking safety standards

quiz.question: The most adaptable employees thrived in the rapidly changing environment. What does 'adaptable' mean?
options: Able to adjust effectively to new conditions
Willing to take on different roles as the need arises
Open-minded and not attached to any particular method
Experienced enough to transfer skills to new contexts
quiz.answer: Able to adjust effectively to new conditions
quiz.explanation:
Adaptable means able to adapt — adjusting successfully to changed circumstances.
• adaptable = able to adjust to new situations
• From Latin: 'adaptare' — to fit
• 'Adaptable species', 'adaptable worker' — able to change without losing effectiveness

quiz.question: The portable generator allowed them to use power tools anywhere on the site. What does 'portable' mean?
options: Able to be carried or moved easily
Compact enough to fit in a standard vehicle
Lightweight despite its high power output
Designed to operate without a fixed power connection
quiz.answer: Able to be carried or moved easily
quiz.explanation:
Portable means easily carried — designed to be moved from place to place.
• portable = able to be carried or moved easily
• From Latin: 'portare' — to carry
• 'Portable device', 'portable skills' — can be taken wherever needed

quiz.question: He found her behavior detestable and refused to be in the same room as her. What does 'detestable' mean?
options: Deserving of strong hatred and contempt
Morally wrong in a way that causes strong disapproval
So offensive that no reasonable person could excuse it
Deliberately provocative and designed to cause offense
quiz.answer: Deserving of strong hatred and contempt
quiz.explanation:
Detestable means hateful — inspiring loathing and strong contempt.
• detestable = extremely unpleasant; deserving to be detested
• From Latin: 'detestari' — to curse, abhor
• Stronger than 'unpleasant' — detestable implies deep moral revulsion

quiz.question: The facts were undisputable — recorded on multiple cameras from different angles. What does 'undisputable' mean?
options: Impossible to challenge or argue against
Too well-documented to be denied by the other side
Admitted as accurate by all parties to the dispute
Supported by evidence from independent sources
quiz.answer: Impossible to challenge or argue against
quiz.explanation:
Undisputable means impossible to dispute — beyond any reasonable challenge.
• undisputable = not open to dispute; unquestionable
• From 'un-' + 'disputable'
• Variant of 'indisputable'; both mean incapable of being disputed

quiz.question: She had an equable temperament that made her ideal for dealing with difficult clients. What does 'equable' mean?
options: Calm and even — not easily disturbed or irritated
Patient and slow to judge in ambiguous situations
Consistently polite and professional regardless of the circumstances
Firm enough to hold her ground without becoming aggressive
quiz.answer: Calm and even — not easily disturbed or irritated
quiz.explanation:
Equable means calm and even — not varying or disturbed by emotion.
• equable = not easily disturbed; steady and even-tempered
• From Latin: 'aequabilis' — uniform
• 'An equable climate' = one with little variation; 'an equable person' = unruffled

quiz.question: The speaker struggled to make himself heard above the noise of the rabble outside. What is a rabble?
options: A disorderly crowd — especially one regarded with contempt
A large group of protesters gathered for a specific cause, as the evidence showed
A mob that has turned violent and is out of control
A noisy group making it impossible for others to be heard
quiz.answer: A disorderly crowd — especially one regarded with contempt
quiz.explanation:
A rabble is a disorderly crowd — used contemptuously to describe the common masses.
• rabble = a disorderly mob; the lowest classes of society
• From Middle Dutch: 'rabbelen' — to chatter
• 'Rabble-rouser' = one who incites a crowd to anger and disorder

quiz.question: He gobbled down his lunch in five minutes and was back at his desk before anyone else. What does 'gobble' mean?
options: To eat quickly and greedily
To eat without pausing to taste or enjoy the food
To eat far more than one actually needs
To eat noisily and without regard for others at the table
quiz.answer: To eat quickly and greedily
quiz.explanation:
To gobble means to eat rapidly and greedily — without pause or pleasure.
• gobble = to eat hurriedly and noisily
• Possibly from the sound of eating; or from 'gob' — the mouth
• 'Gobble up resources' = consume rapidly and completely

quiz.question: After the earthquake, search teams worked through the rubble looking for survivors. What is rubble?
options: Rough broken fragments of brick, stone, or concrete
The remains of a building that has partially collapsed
Debris that has been cleared from a disaster site
Loose material excavated during demolition work
quiz.answer: Rough broken fragments of brick, stone, or concrete
quiz.explanation:
Rubble is the broken debris of demolished or destroyed buildings.
• rubble = rough fragments of brick, stone, or concrete from destroyed structures
• From Old French: 'ruble' — rubble
• 'Reduced to rubble' = completely demolished

quiz.question: Years of illness had enfeebled the old man. What does 'enfeeble' mean?
options: To weaken physically or mentally
To reduce someone to a state of dependency
To deprive someone of their usual strength gradually
To affect both the health and the spirit of a person
quiz.answer: To weaken physically or mentally
quiz.explanation:
To enfeeble means to make feeble — to weaken progressively.
• enfeeble = to make weak; to deprive of strength
• From 'en-' (make) + 'feeble'
• 'Enfeebled by disease', 'an enfeebling restriction' — reducing capacity and strength

quiz.question: The mathematician showed that the fraction was already irreducible. What does 'irreducible' mean?
options: Impossible to simplify further
Already expressed in its lowest possible form
Not divisible by any common factor other than 1
Consisting of numbers that share no common factor
quiz.answer: Impossible to simplify further
quiz.explanation:
Irreducible means that cannot be reduced or simplified further.
• irreducible = not able to be made simpler or smaller
• From Latin: 'ir-' + 'reducere' (to lead back)
• 'An irreducible minimum' = the least possible; 'irreducible fraction' = fully simplified

quiz.question: The witness gave a credible account of what she had seen. What does 'credible' mean?
options: Able to be believed — convincing and trustworthy
Consistent with the other evidence in the case
Detailed enough to suggest a genuine eyewitness
Given confidently and without significant hesitation
quiz.answer: Able to be believed — convincing and trustworthy
quiz.explanation:
Credible means believable — capable of being trusted.
• credible = able to be believed; plausible
• From Latin: 'credibilis' — worthy of belief
• 'A credible threat', 'a credible source' — one that commands belief

quiz.question: Her voice was barely audible above the noise of the crowd. What does 'audible' mean?
options: Able to be heard
Clear enough to be understood
Loud enough to carry across a large space
Distinct enough to be separated from background noise
quiz.answer: Able to be heard
quiz.explanation:
Audible means able to be heard — perceptible to the ear.
• audible = capable of being heard; loud enough to be perceived
• From Latin: 'audire' — to hear
• 'Barely audible' = only just heard; 'audible signal' = one the ear can detect

quiz.question: The prescription was illegible — the pharmacist had to phone the doctor to confirm the dosage. What does 'illegible' mean?
options: Impossible to read
Written too quickly for the letters to be distinct
In handwriting too small to be read without magnification
Faded to the point where the text could not be made out
quiz.answer: Impossible to read
quiz.explanation:
Illegible means impossible to read — so unclear that the text cannot be deciphered.
• illegible = not clear enough to be read
• From Latin: 'il-' + 'legere' (to read)
• Opposite: legible (clear and easy to read)

quiz.question: Only residents of more than five years were eligible to vote in the local election. What does 'eligible' mean?
options: Meeting the required conditions to qualify for something
Registered in the relevant constituency in time
Entitled to vote under the rules of the local authority, as the evidence showed
Fulfilling both the residency and age requirements
quiz.answer: Meeting the required conditions to qualify for something
quiz.explanation:
Eligible means qualified — meeting the requirements to be chosen or to participate.
• eligible = satisfying the conditions; qualified for selection
• From Latin: 'eligere' — to choose
• 'Eligible bachelor' = one considered suitable; 'eligible voter' = meeting the criteria

quiz.question: He was declared ineligible for the final after testing positive for a banned substance. What does 'ineligible' mean?
options: Not qualified or entitled to take part
Disqualified after initially being permitted to compete
Barred from the event because of a rules violation
Excluded on the basis of a positive test result
quiz.answer: Not qualified or entitled to take part
quiz.explanation:
Ineligible means not meeting the requirements — disqualified from participation.
• ineligible = not satisfying the conditions for selection or eligibility
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'eligere' (to choose)
• Direct opposite of eligible

quiz.question: The professor spoke quickly but remained intelligible throughout. What does 'intelligible' mean?
options: Able to be understood
Clear enough to follow despite the speed of delivery
Accessible to a non-specialist audience
Logical and well-structured in its presentation
quiz.answer: Able to be understood
quiz.explanation:
Intelligible means able to be understood — clear enough to comprehend.
• intelligible = capable of being understood
• From Latin: 'intelligibilis' — comprehensible
• Opposite: unintelligible; 'barely intelligible' = just on the edge of comprehension

quiz.question: The teacher called Tom incorrigible — no matter how many times he was corrected, he repeated the same mistakes. What does 'incorrigible' mean?
options: Not able to be reformed or improved by correction
Stubbornly resistant to all forms of discipline
Too careless to notice when something has gone wrong
Likely to repeat the same errors regardless of the help offered
quiz.answer: Not able to be reformed or improved by correction
quiz.explanation:
Incorrigible means beyond correction — too set in bad ways to be changed.
• incorrigible = not capable of being corrected or reformed
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'corrigere' (to correct)
• 'An incorrigible liar' = one who cannot be made to stop; 'incorrigible rogue' = cheerfully unreformable

quiz.question: The new policy had produced no tangible results in the first year. What does 'tangible' mean?
options: Real and concrete — able to be clearly perceived
Measurable against the targets that were originally set
Large enough to be detected in the official statistics
Significant enough to justify the investment already made
quiz.answer: Real and concrete — able to be clearly perceived
quiz.explanation:
Tangible means real and concrete — able to be touched or clearly perceived.
• tangible = perceptible by touch; real and definite
• From Latin: 'tangibilis' — touchable
• 'Tangible results' = real, demonstrable outcomes; 'tangible assets' = physical assets

quiz.question: The brand's value included intangible assets like reputation and customer loyalty. What does 'intangible' mean?
options: Not physical or concrete — difficult to measure or grasp
Too subjective to be assigned a monetary value
Real in its effects but invisible on a balance sheet, as the evidence showed
Valuable but impossible to own or transfer legally
quiz.answer: Not physical or concrete — difficult to measure or grasp
quiz.explanation:
Intangible means not able to be touched or clearly defined — abstract.
• intangible = not tangible; unable to be measured or touched
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'tangibilis'
• 'Intangible assets' = brand value, goodwill; 'an intangible quality' = hard to pin down

quiz.question: The ceremony left an indelible impression on everyone who attended. What does 'indelible' mean?
options: Impossible to remove or forget
So powerful that it affected everyone present differently
Strong enough to be remembered years after the event
Unusual and memorable in a way that was hard to explain
quiz.answer: Impossible to remove or forget
quiz.explanation:
Indelible means permanent — impossible to erase or forget.
• indelible = making marks that cannot be removed; unforgettable
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'delere' (to efface)
• 'Indelible ink', 'indelible memory' — it cannot be wiped out

quiz.question: The judge reminded the jury that all witnesses are fallible. What does 'fallible' mean?
options: Capable of making mistakes or being wrong
Likely to misremember details under pressure
Subject to bias and the distortions of memory
Not always reliable in cross-examination conditions
quiz.answer: Capable of making mistakes or being wrong
quiz.explanation:
Fallible means capable of error — not infallible.
• fallible = liable to be wrong; capable of making mistakes
• From Latin: 'fallere' — to deceive
• Opposite: infallible (incapable of error)

quiz.question: His friends tolerated his foibles — the endless anecdotes and mild obsession with schedules. What are foibles?
options: Minor weaknesses or quirks of character
Personal habits that occasionally annoy others
Endearing eccentricities that define someone's personality
Small flaws that become more pronounced in social settings
quiz.answer: Minor weaknesses or quirks of character
quiz.explanation:
Foibles are minor personal weaknesses or idiosyncrasies.
• foible = a minor weakness or eccentricity; a quirk
• From French: 'foible' — weak
• 'Forgive someone their foibles' = accept their small flaws with affection

quiz.question: The engineer said the tunnel was feasible given the geology and available technology. What does 'feasible' mean?
options: Possible to do — practicable given the conditions
Safe to attempt given the structural analysis
Likely to stay within the budget allowed
Already achieved in similar projects elsewhere, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Possible to do — practicable given the conditions
quiz.explanation:
Feasible means capable of being done — practicable and achievable.
• feasible = possible to do easily; practicable
• From French: 'faisable' — doable
• 'A feasibility study' = an assessment of whether something can be done

quiz.question: The tribunal found the officer's conduct reprehensible and dismissed him. What does 'reprehensible' mean?
options: Deserving strong criticism and moral condemnation
Serious enough to warrant dismissal under the terms of his contract
In deliberate breach of the standards required by his office
So dishonest that it brought the entire institution into disrepute
quiz.answer: Deserving strong criticism and moral condemnation
quiz.explanation:
Reprehensible means deserving censure — conduct that ought to be condemned.
• reprehensible = deserving of reproach; blameworthy
• From Latin: 'reprehendere' — to seize, hold back, blame
• 'Reprehensible behavior' is not just wrong — it invites public condemnation

quiz.question: The manual was rewritten to make it comprehensible to non-technical users. What does 'comprehensible' mean?
options: Able to be understood
Simplified enough for the intended audience to follow
Written at a level suited to a general readership
Organized clearly enough that no training is required
quiz.answer: Able to be understood
quiz.explanation:
Comprehensible means understandable — capable of being grasped.
• comprehensible = intelligible; within the power of someone to understand
• From Latin: 'comprehendere' — to grasp
• Opposite: incomprehensible (impossible to understand)

quiz.question: She made the sensible decision to bring an umbrella despite the morning sunshine. What does 'sensible' mean?
options: Showing practical wisdom and good judgment
Cautious enough to prepare for any eventuality
Aware of the changeable nature of the local weather
Experienced enough to know not to trust an early forecast
quiz.answer: Showing practical wisdom and good judgment
quiz.explanation:
Sensible means showing good practical judgment — rational and sound.
• sensible = having or showing common sense; practical
• From Latin: 'sensibilis' — perceptible by the senses
• 'A sensible choice' = one that reasonable people would endorse

quiz.question: The ostensible purpose of the visit was tourism, but investigators suspected otherwise. What does 'ostensible' mean?
options: Stated or appearing to be the case, but possibly not true
Given as an explanation without any supporting evidence
Plausible on the surface but clearly intended to mislead, as the evidence showed
Officially declared but privately admitted to be false
quiz.answer: Stated or appearing to be the case, but possibly not true
quiz.explanation:
Ostensible means apparently so — professed outwardly, but perhaps not the real truth.
• ostensible = professed; appearing to be the case on the surface
• From Latin: 'ostendere' — to show
• 'Ostensibly' = apparently, on the face of it — implying doubt about the reality

quiz.question: The museum redesigned its displays to make the content accessible to all age groups. What does 'accessible' mean?
options: Easy to understand and engage with
Well-organized and clearly signposted for visitors
Available to anyone who wishes to visit
Suitable for both specialist and general audiences
quiz.answer: Easy to understand and engage with
quiz.explanation:
Accessible means easy to reach or understand — not exclusive or difficult.
• accessible = easy to obtain, use, or understand
• From Latin: 'accedere' — to approach
• 'Accessible design' = usable by everyone; 'accessible writing' = easy to understand

quiz.question: She had an irrepressible enthusiasm that no setback could dampen. What does 'irrepressible' mean?
options: Impossible to hold back or suppress
Maintained cheerfully even in the most difficult circumstances
More intense than most people could sustain
Genuine rather than performed for others' benefit
quiz.answer: Impossible to hold back or suppress
quiz.explanation:
Irrepressible means impossible to suppress — bursting out regardless of circumstances.
• irrepressible = not able to be restrained; impossible to hold back
• From Latin: 'ir-' + 'reprimere' (to press back)
• 'Irrepressible laughter', 'irrepressible spirit' — it cannot be contained

quiz.question: The suspect's alibi was plausible but unverified. What does 'plausible' mean?
options: Seeming reasonable and likely to be true
Consistent with the known facts but not yet confirmed
Hard to disprove without additional investigation
Presented confidently and in a convincing manner
quiz.answer: Seeming reasonable and likely to be true
quiz.explanation:
Plausible means seeming probable — having the appearance of truth or reason.
• plausible = apparently reasonable or truthful
• From Latin: 'plausibilis' — deserving applause
• 'A plausible excuse' seems believable; plausibility ≠ truth

quiz.question: The defendant's explanation was implausible — it defied all common sense. What does 'implausible' mean?
options: Not seeming reasonable or likely to be true
Inconsistent with the evidence that had been established
Too convenient to be accepted without corroboration
Delivered in a way that suggested it had been fabricated
quiz.answer: Not seeming reasonable or likely to be true
quiz.explanation:
Implausible means not plausible — unlikely and hard to believe.
• implausible = not seeming reasonable or probable
• From Latin: 'im-' + 'plausibilis'
• Opposite of plausible; 'an implausible story' strains credibility

quiz.question: The two software systems were not compatible — data could not be transferred between them. What does 'compatible' mean?
options: Able to work or exist together without conflict
Designed to the same technical specifications
Produced by the same manufacturer or ecosystem
Capable of exchanging data in a standard format, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Able to work or exist together without conflict
quiz.explanation:
Compatible means able to work together — without conflict or incompatibility.
• compatible = capable of coexisting or working together
• From Latin: 'compati' — to suffer together; to be in sympathy
• 'Compatible values', 'compatible systems' — they fit and work together

quiz.question: Their working styles were incompatible — she needed structure and he thrived in chaos. What does 'incompatible' mean?
options: Unable to exist or work together without conflict
So different that collaboration would always be difficult
Likely to cause friction in any shared environment
Productive individually but unable to cooperate effectively
quiz.answer: Unable to exist or work together without conflict
quiz.explanation:
Incompatible means unable to work together — fundamentally at odds.
• incompatible = not able to exist or work together without conflict
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'compati'
• Direct opposite of compatible

quiz.question: The manufacturer claimed the toy was nearly indestructible, but a child proved it was very much destructible within five minutes. What does 'destructible' mean?
options: Able to be broken or destroyed
Designed for rough use but not genuinely indestructible
Likely to break if subjected to more force than intended
Made from materials that eventually degrade with use
quiz.answer: Able to be broken or destroyed
quiz.explanation:
Destructible means capable of being destroyed — not indestructible.
• destructible = able to be destroyed
• From Latin: 'destruere' — to pull down
• Opposite: indestructible

quiz.question: Children are particularly susceptible to the disease, which is why vaccination is prioritized for them. What does 'susceptible' mean?
options: Liable to be affected — lacking resistance to something
More likely to develop a serious form of the illness
Unable to benefit from the immunity others acquire naturally
At greater risk because their immune systems are still developing
quiz.answer: Liable to be affected — lacking resistance to something
quiz.explanation:
Susceptible means easily affected or influenced — lacking the protection to resist.
• susceptible = likely to be influenced or harmed; vulnerable
• From Latin: 'suscipere' — to take up
• 'Susceptible to disease', 'susceptible to flattery' — open and unguarded

quiz.question: The court called his denial of all responsibility contemptible. What does 'contemptible' mean?
options: Deserving contempt — causing disgust and scorn
Morally unacceptable given the severity of the harm caused
Deliberately dishonest in a way that insulted the court
An attempt to avoid justice by denying undeniable facts
quiz.answer: Deserving contempt — causing disgust and scorn
quiz.explanation:
Contemptible means deserving of contempt — worthy of scorn and disgust.
• contemptible = despicable; worthy of contempt
• From Latin: 'contemnere' — to despise
• Distinct from 'contemptuous' (showing contempt): contemptible = what is scorned; contemptuous = the attitude of scorning

quiz.question: The judge was widely regarded as incorruptible — no amount of money had ever swayed him. What does 'incorruptible' mean?
options: Impossible to corrupt through bribery or pressure
Too principled to be persuaded by financial incentives
Guided by integrity alone, regardless of personal cost
Known for having refused multiple offers of inducement
quiz.answer: Impossible to corrupt through bribery or pressure
quiz.explanation:
Incorruptible means not able to be corrupted — immune to bribery or moral decay.
• incorruptible = not susceptible to corruption
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'corrumpere' (to corrupt)
• 'An incorruptible official' is one no inducement can buy

quiz.question: The startup's investors chose convertible notes instead of equity. What does 'convertible' mean here?
options: Able to be changed into a different form
Flexible enough to suit different stages of a company
Designed to minimize risk for early-stage investors
Exchangeable for equity at a later date under agreed terms
quiz.answer: Able to be changed into a different form
quiz.explanation:
Convertible means able to be converted — changed from one form to another.
• convertible = capable of being changed into a different form or use
• From Latin: 'convertere' — to turn around
• 'Convertible car' = roof opens; 'convertible bond' = can be converted to equity

quiz.question: The DNA evidence was incontrovertible — it placed him at the scene beyond any doubt. What does 'incontrovertible' mean?
options: Impossible to dispute or argue against
Conclusive enough to end any remaining doubt
Stronger than any other piece of evidence in the case
Accepted by all parties as accurate and reliable
quiz.answer: Impossible to dispute or argue against
quiz.explanation:
Incontrovertible means impossible to dispute — beyond any argument.
• incontrovertible = not able to be denied or disputed
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'controvertere' (to turn against)
• 'Incontrovertible proof' cannot be refuted; the case is closed

quiz.question: The explorer's pack included a selection of comestibles for the three-day trek. What are comestibles?
options: Articles of food — things fit to be eaten
Provisions needed to survive in harsh conditions
Lightweight foodstuffs chosen for their caloric density
Preserved foods designed to last without refrigeration
quiz.answer: Articles of food — things fit to be eaten
quiz.explanation:
Comestibles are food items — things that are edible.
• comestibles = items of food; things fit to eat
• From Latin: 'comestus' — eaten up
• A slightly formal or humorous word for food provisions

quiz.question: She seemed to have an inexhaustible supply of energy, never tiring throughout the day. What does 'inexhaustible' mean?
options: Impossible to use up or deplete
Consistently maintained at a high level
Renewed overnight so she was always fresh
Greater than anyone else around her
quiz.answer: Impossible to use up or deplete
quiz.explanation:
Inexhaustible means not able to be exhausted — too great ever to run out.
• inexhaustible = not capable of being used up
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'exhaustus' (drained)
• 'Inexhaustible resources', 'inexhaustible patience' — it never runs out

quiz.question: Combustible materials must be stored away from any heat source. What does 'combustible' mean?
options: Capable of catching fire and burning
Reactive when exposed to oxygen at high temperature
Likely to ignite spontaneously under certain conditions
Classified as hazardous under fire safety regulations
quiz.answer: Capable of catching fire and burning
quiz.explanation:
Combustible means able to catch fire and burn — flammable.
• combustible = capable of catching fire; burning readily
• From Latin: 'comburere' — to burn up
• Also figurative: 'a combustible situation' = one likely to explode into conflict

quiz.question: Expanding into the new market was a gamble that paid off handsomely. What is a gamble?
options: A risky action taken in the hope of a favorable outcome
An investment made without full knowledge of the market, as the evidence showed
A bold decision that could succeed or fail dramatically
A venture judged more likely to fail than to succeed
quiz.answer: A risky action taken in the hope of a favorable outcome
quiz.explanation:
A gamble is a risky action — something done without certainty of success, hoping for gain.
• gamble = to take a risky action; to bet on an uncertain outcome
• From Old English: 'gamenian' — to play
• 'It paid off' = the gamble succeeded; 'a calculated gamble' = risk accepted knowingly

quiz.question: The jazz ensemble performed three sets in the intimate venue. What is an ensemble?
options: A group of musicians performing together
A band that plays a specific style of music
A small group that performs without a conductor
A collection of musicians who have played together for years
quiz.answer: A group of musicians performing together
quiz.explanation:
An ensemble is a group performing together — in music, theatre, or fashion.
• ensemble = a group of musicians or performers working together
• From French: 'ensemble' — together
• Also: an 'ensemble cast' = all actors of equal importance; a clothing 'ensemble' = a coordinated outfit

quiz.question: The students were told to assemble in the hall at 9am. What does 'assemble' mean?
options: To gather together in one place
To sit in rows in the order specified by the teachers
To register their attendance before going to class
To arrive promptly for a formal school occasion
quiz.answer: To gather together in one place
quiz.explanation:
To assemble means to gather together — to bring or come together in one place.
• assemble = to gather; to collect or bring together
• From Latin: 'assimulare' — to bring together
• 'Assemble a team', 'assemble flat-pack furniture' — both involve bringing parts together

quiz.question: The negotiator suspected his counterpart was dissembling — agreeing to terms she had no intention of honoring. What does 'dissemble' mean?
options: To conceal one's true motives behind a false appearance
To negotiate in bad faith by misrepresenting one's position
To agree to conditions that one knows cannot be met
To mislead through carefully chosen ambiguous language
quiz.answer: To conceal one's true motives behind a false appearance
quiz.explanation:
To dissemble means to conceal one's real intentions — to hide behind a false front.
• dissemble = to hide true motives or feelings behind a false appearance
• From Latin: 'dissimulare' — to disguise
• Distinct from 'disassemble' (to take apart) — dissemble is about deceptive concealment

quiz.question: The nimble young dancer moved through the routine without a single misstep. What does 'nimble' mean?
options: Quick and light in movement — agile
Precisely trained to perform complex choreography
Flexible enough to achieve extreme physical positions
Alert and coordinated in ways that come from years of practice
quiz.answer: Quick and light in movement — agile
quiz.explanation:
Nimble means quick and light — agile and quick-moving.
• nimble = quick and light in movement; agile
• From Old English: 'næmel' — quick to grasp
• 'Nimble fingers', 'nimble mind' — quickness and dexterity

quiz.question: He bumbled his way through the presentation, losing his place and dropping his notes. What does 'bumble' mean?
options: To act in a clumsy, confused manner
To speak unclearly when nervous or unprepared
To fail to make an impact despite good intentions
To fumble physically when trying to manage materials
quiz.answer: To act in a clumsy, confused manner
quiz.explanation:
To bumble means to act in a clumsy, confused, or blundering way.
• bumble = to blunder; to act awkwardly and without direction
• From 'boom' or 'hum' — the buzzing of a bumble bee (erratic movement)
• 'Bumble along' = muddle through; 'bumble about' = move clumsily

quiz.question: Despite her extraordinary success, she remained humble and never boasted. What does 'humble' mean?
options: Not arrogant — modest about one's own abilities
Grateful for the opportunities she had been given
Aware that others had contributed to her achievements
Reluctant to discuss her successes in public
quiz.answer: Not arrogant — modest about one's own abilities
quiz.explanation:
Humble means modest — not having or showing arrogance about oneself.
• humble = having a low or modest estimate of one's importance
• From Latin: 'humilis' — low, ground level
• 'A humble person' does not think themselves better than others; 'humble origins' = modest beginnings

quiz.question: The politician left office under ignoble circumstances — forced out by scandal. What does 'ignoble' mean?
options: Dishonourable and not worthy of respect
Embarrassing enough to end a political career
The result of choices that he now deeply regretted
Widely reported and impossible to deny or escape
quiz.answer: Dishonourable and not worthy of respect
quiz.explanation:
Ignoble means not noble — dishonorable, mean, or base.
• ignoble = not honorable; of low character
• From Latin: 'ignobilis' — of low birth or character
• 'An ignoble defeat', 'an ignoble act' — failing to meet the standards of honor

quiz.question: Great literature can ennoble the reader, expanding their sense of what is human. What does 'ennoble' mean?
options: To elevate morally or intellectually
To inspire an interest in the lives of others
To expand the range of emotions one can understand
To improve one's use of language through exposure to great writing
quiz.answer: To elevate morally or intellectually
quiz.explanation:
To ennoble means to make noble — to elevate in character or spirit.
• ennoble = to elevate; to give a higher quality or character to
• From 'en-' + 'noble'
• 'Ennobled by suffering', 'ennobled by great art' — lifted toward what is finest in humanity

quiz.question: The palace floors were laid with Italian marble. What is marble?
options: A hard crystalline limestone used in sculpture and architecture
A polished granite quarried in the Italian Alps
A decorative stone formed from compressed volcanic ash
A type of white stone particularly prized for its smoothness, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A hard crystalline limestone used in sculpture and architecture
quiz.explanation:
Marble is a metamorphic rock — crystalline limestone transformed by heat and pressure.
• marble = a hard rock formed from limestone under heat and pressure
• From Greek: 'marmaros' — shining stone
• Used for sculpture (Michelangelo's David) and architecture (the Parthenon)

quiz.question: The thrush warbled from the top of the apple tree. What does 'warble' mean?
options: To sing with a succession of trilling or quavering notes
To call repeatedly from a fixed position in a tree
To produce the melodious song characteristic of thrushes, as the evidence showed
To sing in a pattern that changes with the time of day
quiz.answer: To sing with a succession of trilling or quavering notes
quiz.explanation:
To warble means to sing with trilling, melodious, undulating notes.
• warble = to sing with trills and runs; to quaver melodiously
• From Old Norse: 'hvarbla' — to twitter
• 'Warbling brook', 'warbling bird' — liquid, fluctuating sound

quiz.question: Sugar is soluble in water. What does 'soluble' mean?
options: Able to be dissolved in a liquid
Broken down by water into its component parts
Capable of mixing with a liquid without settling
Absorbed into the bloodstream when consumed with liquid
quiz.answer: Able to be dissolved in a liquid
quiz.explanation:
Soluble means able to dissolve — to be dissolved into a solution.
• soluble = capable of being dissolved
• From Latin: 'solvere' — to loosen, dissolve
• 'Water-soluble vitamins', 'soluble fiber' — capable of dissolving in water

quiz.question: Oil is insoluble in water, which is why they separate into layers. What does 'insoluble' mean?
options: Unable to be dissolved in a liquid
Incapable of mixing with water under any conditions
Lighter than water and therefore always floating on top
Chemically incompatible with water molecules
quiz.answer: Unable to be dissolved in a liquid
quiz.explanation:
Insoluble means not able to dissolve — resistant to dissolution.
• insoluble = not capable of being dissolved
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'solvere'
• Also figurative: 'an insoluble problem' = one that cannot be solved

quiz.question: He was voluble on the subject of local history, speaking without pause for forty minutes. What does 'voluble' mean?
options: Talking fluently and at great length
Deeply knowledgeable and willing to share that knowledge
Enthusiastic enough to speak without needing prompting
So well-informed that he could answer any question asked
quiz.answer: Talking fluently and at great length
quiz.explanation:
Voluble means talking a great deal — fluent and continuous in speech.
• voluble = talking at length and with ease; garrulous
• From Latin: 'volvere' — to roll (the words roll out)
• 'A voluble speaker' flows continuously; can imply too much talking

quiz.question: The election campaign ended in a complete debacle — the candidate lost by a record margin. What is a debacle?
options: A sudden, complete, and humiliating collapse or disaster
An unexpectedly large defeat in a competitive contest
A series of errors that culminated in an embarrassing result
A failure that was widely predicted but still surprising in scale
quiz.answer: A sudden, complete, and humiliating collapse or disaster
quiz.explanation:
A debacle is a sudden, total, and humiliating failure — a complete collapse.
• debacle = a sudden disastrous defeat; a complete shambles
• From French: 'débâcle' — a breaking up of ice; a sudden catastrophic rush
• 'The whole thing was a debacle' = a total and embarrassing disaster
